points: A magnet has two poles: North (N) and South (S), which always exist in pairs and cannot be isolated.
Magnetic materials (Iron, Nickel, Cobalt) are attracted to magnets, while non-magnetic materials (Wood, Plastic) are not.
Repulsion between two magnets is the only sure test to identify a magnet.
A freely suspended magnet always rests in the North-South direction, used historically for navigation with devices like the 'matsya-yantra'.
A magnetic compass uses a magnetized needle to indicate direction, aligning itself with Earth's magnetic field.
Like poles (N-N, S-S) repel each other, while unlike poles (N-S) attract each other.
Magnetic forces can act through non-magnetic materials like glass, water, and cardboard.
Magnets must be stored in pairs with unlike poles together and iron 'keepers' to prevent demagnetization.
